item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of how the Planet Express ship works, except that this skip changes history instead of having the world or universe move for it.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Currently, you have an unsourced image within your article. Please source this image by replying to the sent PM and/or this post within 48 hours or they will have to be deleted. Also, make sure the image complies with the SCP Wiki's CC-BY-SA 3.0 license by following this handy guide, otherwise it will have to be deleted.

48 hours have elapsed, and thus, the image(s) have been removed. Please PM with the source if you wish to have them restored or if you have any questions. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just want to point something else: this is just a theory, but I think one reason why they can't just kill the cat is because it would not just make it dead, but it would alter all of history to make the cat have never been alive in the first place, nullifying all changes that it had ever made to the timeline. Here are some potential consequences:

- The Foundation is not created with the cat as one of the first objects in containment, irreversibly altering their history in a potentially catastrophic way.

- The Earth's continents are altered, potentially retroactively erasing many sections of humanity.

- The cat takes extreme measures to defend itself, altering history to make its restraints ineffective and potentially killing incalculably many people while it runs away.

So not only does the Foundation have to keep the cat from moving, they ALSO have to keep it from being harmed in a breach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this piece! The concept is awesome (and as a cat owner I absolutely relate), and the execution lives up to it!

One thing that might be cool to include is some examples in the movement logs of more significant changes made to human history. The fact that this cat can alter geologic history by throwing a meteor at the Earth millions of years in the past is both hilarious and a great way to demonstrate how powerful this thing is, but it would be cool to see it move itself by impacting significant human events as well- changing the results of elections, that sort of thing. A cat that can move tectonic plates is scary one one level because it's so powerful, but tectonic plates are pretty removed from everyday experience. A cat that changes who won WWII is scary in a very different and also interesting way. Just my thoughts though - don't mean to stick my nose into the creative aspects of someone else's story when it doesn't have any problems in the first place.

Again, super creative and well-done SCP! Definitely a new favorite!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a huge compliment, thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This fucking cat… As someone who used to own a cat (who was the laziest goddamn being on this planet) this hit home for me and left me roaring. Absolutely wonderful! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Further testing has revealed the subject loves lasagna but hates Mondays…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was definitely struggling with how exactly the Foundation would know about the cat's effects, I'll need to fix that up somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The description is much better and the logs are hilarious.

LAzy CaTs GOnNa gET us All KiLLed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the idea of a cat that's just way too lazy to move, they manipulate history instead of moving their lazy ass is amusing as shit.

Seconded wholeheartedly, that's a goddamn riot. But there doesn't seem to be any reason the foundation should be able to know that this is the case. SCP-1780 is one of the few that are able to write around the subject in a manner that feels sensible. Here it feels like it's built off direct tells from the author, weighing pretty heavily on suspension of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've fixed all the concerns you brought up. I was actually on holiday when I wrote this so posting it on Drafts and Critiques slipped my mind, thanks for bringing that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've replaced a lot of things with logs of SCP-3487 events to tighten up the tone a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For example, if SCP-3487 “moves” 3 metres to the right, it does not change location in space, but may instead add a meteor impact into history such that the rotational speed of the Earth changes, resulting in the ground under it being displaced 3 metres to the left in the present day.

Love the concept, but this sentence seems to be written more casually than the rest of the article. If some of the wording in the description is tightened up, then the overall article will rest better with me. Tentative upvote, but pretty good nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Naw, kitten :D.

This was clever, lovely, and pleasantly low-key. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Such a cool concept and even though the writing is slightly off (which has been written by others so I won't bother saying) it is still great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Author, you have a very solid concept here. I personally enjoy it. However, I think I'm going to leave this at a no-vote for now. There are a few things that you could do to polish up this work.

+ show block
– hide block

Though you don't have any run-on sentences, there are a few sentences that are incredibly long. Your last paragraph for example:

These historical changes cause the butterfly effect, and though this effect appears to be somewhat muted (considering the expected results of some of these changes), there remains a significant risk of a CK-class restructuring event caused by SCP-3487, with an immeasurable number assumed to have already taken place before containment to establish present-day consensus reality.

is all one sentence. It can be a bit of a slog to read, and breaking it up would help your article flow easier.

SCP-3487’s primary anomalous property manifests when it moves, whether by walking or by utilising man-made transport (such as cars and elevators).

This passage confused me a bit, particularly the latter part. Especially since at the bottom you say

The anomalous effects of SCP-3487 do not manifest if it is not moving of its own volition, and thus it can be transported without issue.

which is a bit contradictory, from what I can tell.

SCP-3487 can be harmed in a similar manner to other house cats, though it heals at a slightly accelerated rate producing no scar tissue and is able to slowly regrow parts of itself (including vital organs), resulting in it being effectively immortal.

While this gives me the impression that the Foundation took apart this poor lazy kitty, it is a little loose on its tone. A suggestion I might make:

SCP-3487 is not impervious to physical harm, although it is capable of accelerated regeneration. Therefore, it is theorized that SCP-3487 cannot die.

Out of curiosity, did you get this looked over in the Drafts and Critique forums? It may take a few days for people to respond, but someone always responds and it is a useful resource for getting work to its best possible state. It may be worth your time to consider this in the event that your article doesn't succeed. Again, I think you have a novel concept here and some of the writing isn't bad, but it is just a little ways out of upvote territory for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What suggestions do you have for tightening up the tone? I'm definitely noticing what you mean I'm just unsure of what exactly to change other than making some "big word substitutions", which isn't really how to establish a clinical tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is cat laziness taken to a whole new level. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone could use some tightening up in some places, but the idea of a cat that's just way too lazy to move, they manipulate history instead of moving their lazy ass is amusing as shit.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first Keter class SCP. I didn't go into this looking to write a Keter (though I had wanted to for a while), but the idea of moving the world around yourself suddenly came to me one day and I expanded on that, and now here we are!

Hope you enjoyed this.
